verbatim: [§24] It will not have porticos nor baths of your providing? And what signifies that? Why, neither does a smith provide it with shoes, nor a shoemaker with arms. It is enough if every one fully performs his own proper business.
reasoning: The text outlines that specific material contributions to the state (baths/porticos) are not the 'business' of private citizens or specific tradesmen, categorizing these resources by occupational role.
rule_polity_reasoning: The rule discusses the division of labor and public works (porticos, baths) common in Roman society, where Epictetus lived.
rule_time_reasoning: The rule reflects the social organization and expectations of citizens in the Roman Empire during Epictetus's lifetime.
author: Epictetus

verbatim: [§133] Instances of this are the pyramids in Egypt and the votive offerings of the Cypselids... and the temples at Samos... (for all these undertakings produce the same effect, constant occupation and poverty among the subject people).
reasoning: The ruling power uses large-scale construction projects to ensure subjects remain in a state of 'constant occupation,' depriving them of the leisure necessary for political plotting.
rule_polity: Ancient Near East / Classical Greek tyrannies
rule_polity_reasoning: The rule references historical examples like the pyramids in Egypt and votive offerings of the Cypselids, linking them to the practice of tyrants keeping subjects occupied and poor.
rule_time_reasoning: The rule references past historical examples (Egypt, Cypselids) to illustrate a principle applicable to contemporary tyrannies.
author: Aristotle
description: 4th-century BCE Classical Greek philosopher and polymath

verbatim: [§1202] Moreover the society of the good may supply a sort of training in goodness, as Theognis remarks. Again, if we examine the matter more fundamentally, it appears that a virtuous friend is essentially desirable for a virtuous man.
reasoning: Aristotle identifies a specific class of people—the virtuous (a group defined by their moral education/character)—and argues they have a unique need and access to the 'greatest of external goods': friends of similar character.
rule_polity_reasoning: The rule discusses universal ethical principles of friendship and virtue, not tied to a specific historical polity.
rule_time_reasoning: The concept of virtuous friendship is presented as a timeless ethical principle, applicable across different eras.
author: Aristotle
description: 4th-century BCE Classical Greek philosopher and polymath

verbatim: [§147] Moreover the characteristics of a tyranny also are all thought to be democratic, I mean for instance licence among slaves, which may really be advantageous for the popular party up to a point, and among women and children, and indulgence to live as one likes.
reasoning: Aristotle notes that in certain types of democracy, legal or social norms allow slaves a higher degree of freedom from restraint ('licence') than in other regimes.
rule_polity_reasoning: The rule describes 'licence among slaves, women and children' as a characteristic of radical democracy, implying a specific social condition in some Greek polities.
rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle is observing and critiquing social phenomena he associates with contemporary radical democracies.
author: Aristotle
description: 4th-century BCE Classical Greek philosopher and polymath

verbatim: [§18] [17] PART 17 Now such of the younger men as have dignified themselves with the names of these two authorities by taking the appellations Erasistrateans or Asclepiadeans are like the Davi and Getae — the slaves introduced by the excellent Menander into his comedies. As these slaves held that they had done nothing fine unless they had cheated their master three times, so also the men I am discussing have taken their time over the construction of impudent sophisms
reasoning: Galen references a contemporary cultural norm and literary convention where slaves (specifically Davi and Getae in New Comedy) are defined by their status as deceitful actors against their masters, stripping them of the resource of being perceived as honest or virtuous.
rule_polity_reasoning: The rule explicitly references characters from Menander's comedies, which are set in Classical Athens.
rule_time_reasoning: The rule refers to characters from Menander's comedies, which were written centuries before Galen's time, placing the reference in the past.
author: Galen
occupations: physician; philosopher; physician writer; neuroscientist; surgeon; biologist

rule: Metic lack of land ownership rights
verbatim: [§140] I say nothing of the wealth that lies before your eyes—houses, timber, grain; but in the country of our ruined allies there are estates and extensive farms bringing in a rental of a talent to Philocrates and half a talent to Aeschines. [§141] Surely, men of Athens, it is strange and intolerable that the disasters of your allies have become the emolument of your envoys, and that one and the same peace should have brought, to the city sending ambassadors, the destruction of allies, dispossession of property... and to the ambassadors themselves who intrigued against the city, revenues, property, estates, and opulence in exchange for penury.
reasoning: The text contrasts the deprivation of property from allies (non-citizens of the localized territory) with the acquisition of estates by Athenian citizen ambassadors. It demonstrates that while citizens can hold property and revenues, non-citizens (allies in this context) were dispossessed, establishing the boundary of property rights based on political status.
rule_polity_reasoning: The rule implies that non-citizens (metics or foreigners) could not own land in Athens, a known restriction in Classical Athenian law.
rule_time_reasoning: Demosthenes is describing a contemporary legal and social reality in Athens regarding land ownership.
author: Demosthenes
description: classical Athenian statesman and orator (384–322 BC)

verbatim: [§33] Those physicians who have not judgment combined with their dexterity, expose themselves in fractures of the jaws, as in other cases, for they apply a variety of bandages to a fractured jaw-bone... For all such bandaging of a fractured jawbone has a tendency rather to derange the bones connected with the fracture, than to bring them into their natural position.
reasoning: The text defines a professional group (physicians) and notes how those lacking 'judgment' lose the resource of professional efficacy and reputation by applying harmful treatments that derange the bones.
rule_polity_reasoning: The rule discusses professional judgment and medical efficacy, which are universal principles of medical practice rather than specific to a polity.
rule_time_reasoning: The concept of professional judgment and effective medical practice is a timeless principle, not limited to a particular historical era.
author: Hippocrates

verbatim: [§83] this is why in ancient times there were oligarchies in all the states whose strength lay in their cavalry, and they used to use horses for their wars against their neighbors, as for instance did the Eretrians and Chalcidians and the people of Magnesia on the Maeander
reasoning: The text explains that in historical Greek states, the occupation or capacity to maintain cavalry (linked to wealth) determined the form of government (oligarchy).
rule_polity_reasoning: The rule explicitly mentions ancient times and specific Greek city-states (Eretrians, Chalcidians, Magnesia) known for their cavalry-based oligarchies.
rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle refers to 'ancient times' and specific historical examples of Greek city-states that had cavalry-based oligarchies, indicating a past era relative to his floruit.
author: Aristotle
description: 4th-century BCE Classical Greek philosopher and polymath
